title: Dog Eating Rocks or Dirt (Pica): Causes, Risks, and Triage Guide
meta_desc: Understand why dogs eat rocks or dirt (pica), the risks of intestinal blockage, and how to triage your pet's condition with a veterinarian.
content_html:
TL;DR: Pica is the ingestion of non-food items like rocks, which can be caused by medical or psychological issues and can lead to life-threatening intestinal blockages. If your dog shows signs of distress like vomiting or a painful abdomen after eating rocks, contact an emergency veterinarian immediately.

What is pica in dogs and why is my pet eating non-food items?

Pica is a medical term used to describe the craving and ingestion of non-food items such as rocks, dirt, clay, or fabric. In dogs, this behavior can be driven by psychological factors like boredom or anxiety, or underlying medical issues such as nutritional deficiencies, anemia, or gastrointestinal disorders. While it may seem like a simple bad habit, it often indicates your pet is seeking something missing in their diet or environment.

Is it a medical emergency if my dog eats rocks?

Urgency Level: Medium. While eating a small amount of dirt may not require a trip to the ER, ingesting rocks is a serious concern. You should contact an emergency veterinarian immediately if your dog displays any of the following:

  • Vomiting or lethargy
  • A painful abdomen
  • An inability to pass stool
  • Signs of life-threatening intestinal blockages, choke hazards, or internal perforations

How does taking a photo of what my dog ate help the veterinarian?

Taking a photo of the material your dog has been eating—or even a photo of their stool if they have already passed some of the material—is incredibly helpful for veterinary triage. A clear image allows the vet to:

  • Assess the size, shape, and sharpness of the ingested objects.
  • Determine if the item is likely to pass naturally through the digestive tract.
  • Decide if surgical intervention is required to prevent internal damage.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Behavioral problems related to ingestion include pica, the consumption of nonfood items. Although some dogs with pica also exhibit polyphagia or compulsive disorders, many dogs, especially puppies, may begin to chew and ingest nonfood items as part of investigative and exploratory behavior. Medical causes for pica should be excluded first. Management strategies to prevent access to potential targets are needed when the dog cannot be supervised.

Chapter: Behavioral

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 1568)

faqs.question: Can nutritional deficiencies cause my dog to eat dirt?
faqs.answer: Yes, dogs may eat dirt or clay if they are lacking specific minerals in their diet or if they have an underlying condition like anemia that causes them to seek out iron-rich soil.

faqs.question: What are the signs of an intestinal blockage from rocks?
faqs.answer: Common signs include repeated vomiting, loss of appetite, straining to defecate, lethargy, and a visible expression of pain when the belly is touched.

faqs.question: How can I stop my dog from eating rocks?
faqs.answer: Prevention involves close supervision during walks, clearing the yard of small stones, and addressing the root cause through behavioral training or a veterinary check-up to rule out medical issues.
